reasoning: The finding claims arbitrary option write via attacker-controlled notice_name, but misses the nonce check on line 77: check_ajax_referer($optionName, self::POST_FIELD_SECURITY). The nonce action is 'wpdesk_notice_dismiss_' + $noticeName. Per PermanentDismissibleNotice.php line 45, wp_create_nonce($this->noticeDismissOptionName) is called only when a notice is server-side instantiated and displayed. To pass check_ajax_referer for an arbitrary $noticeName, the attacker must supply a valid WordPress nonce for the action string 'wpdesk_notice_dismiss_<arbitrary>'. WordPress nonces are server-generated HMAC tokens tied to user session; an authenticated user cannot fabricate one for an action string that was never passed to wp_create_nonce() on the server. No public endpoint exists to generate arbitrary-action nonces. Therefore, the only option names writable via this handler are those for notices that were legitimately instantiated and displayed to the requesting user — exactly the intended behavior (dismiss a notice). The option name space is further constrained to the 'wpdesk_notice_dismiss_' prefix, which contains only notice dismissal flags with boolean ('1') values. No meaningful privilege escalation or plugin setting corruption is possible. The vulnerability is not real: the nonce check acts as a cryptographic allowlist defeating the arbitrary-write claim.
